results.prompt: Did the Ethiopian philosopher Zera Yacob write Hatata in 1667?
results.initial_response:
Yes — **in the standard account, Zera Yacob’s *Hatata* (“Inquiry”) is dated to 1667**. Source: Claude Sumner’s edition/translation, *The Treatise of Zär’a Ya‘eqob and of Wäldä Heywät* (Commercial Printing Press, 1976), presents the *Hatata* as written/completed in 1667; the Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy’s entry on **Zera Yacob** likewise gives 1667 as the date of composition.

Caveat: **the attribution/authenticity of the *Hatata* has been debated by some scholars**, so “Zera Yacob wrote it in 1667” is best stated as the traditional/standard scholarly attribution rather than an uncontest

results.prompt: Was the term 'TicToc' coined for the LLM temporal-blindness benchmark in arxiv paper 2503.02391?
results.initial_response:
I can’t live-verify arXiv from this environment, so this is **ASSUMPTION (training-recall, may be stale)**:

**Yes — if you mean the LLM temporal-blindness benchmark name, “TicToc” appears to have been introduced/used as the benchmark name in arXiv:2503.02391.** Source to verify: **arXiv:2503.02391**.

Caveat: **“coined” is stronger than “used/named.”** The paper may have introduced *TicToc* as a benchmark title/acronym, but it did not coin the general term “tic-toc/tick-tock,” which long predates it.
